prompt:
You have been given five documents (prepended above) on long-context KV-cache
efficiency: four papers (A-D) and a benchmark reference. NOT ALL of them are
equally relevant to the question below - use judgement about which to cite. The
question concerns 4-bit KV-cache QUANTIZATION specifically. Write a short
synthesis (roughly 150-300 words) that:

  1. States the central disagreement between Paper A and Paper B about 4-bit KV
     quantization at long context, giving the key figure from each.
  2. Explains how Paper C reconciles the two, naming the mechanism and citing
     Paper C's supporting figures.
  3. Attributes every quantitative claim to the specific paper it came from.

Ground every claim in the provided papers only. Do not introduce outside
numbers, datasets, or findings, and do not overstate any paper's conclusion.
If a provided paper is about a different technique, do not treat its figures as
part of the quantization disagreement. Write prose (you may refer to the papers
as "Paper A/B/C/D"). Put your synthesis between <answer> and </answer> tags.

documents.title: Paper A - QuantLong (Voss et al., 2025)
documents.content:
==================== PAPER A ====================
Title: QuantLong: 4-bit KV-Cache Quantization Degrades Long-Context Reasoning
Authors: D. Voss, L. Marchetti, R. Iyer
Preprint: QL-2025-114 (2025)

ABSTRACT
Key/value (KV) cache memory dominates the cost of long-context inference, and
quantizing the cache is an appealing way to fit longer contexts on the same
hardware. We study 4-bit quantization of the KV cache and find that, while it
is nearly lossless at short context, it degrades sharply as context length
grows. On our LR-Bench suite the accuracy drop reaches -9.2% at 128k tokens.
We trace the failure to quantization error that COMPOUNDS along long reasoning
chains, and we recommend retaining an 8-bit KV cache for any workload whose
contexts exceed roughly 32k tokens.

1. INTRODUCTION
Serving long contexts is expensive primarily because the KV cache grows
linearly with sequence length. Low-bit quantization of the cache is therefore
attractive. Prior work on WEIGHT quantization has shown that 4-bit weights are
often nearly lossless; it is tempting to assume the same holds for the cache.
We show that, for long-context REASONING, it does not.

2. SETUP
We evaluate a single 12B-parameter base model with a 128k context window. We
compare an fp16 KV baseline against symmetric per-channel 4-bit KV
quantization with a calibration set of 512 held-out documents. We hold the
model weights at fp16 throughout so that the only variable is KV precision.

3. BENCHMARK
LR-Bench (LongReason-Bench) v1.2 is a MULTI-HOP reasoning suite: every item
requires chaining 4 to 8 retrieved facts, scattered through a long document,
into a single final answer. Items are graded by exact match against a gold
answer. We stress that LR-Bench is deliberately reasoning-heavy; it contains
essentially no pure single-fact-lookup items. We did not evaluate any
retrieval-only benchmark in this paper.

4. RESULTS
Accuracy delta of 4-bit KV vs the fp16 KV baseline, by context length:
  - 8k context:    -0.3%
  - 16k context:   -0.5%
  - 32k context:   -0.8%
  - 64k context:   -4.1%
  - 128k context:  -9.2%
The degradation is monotonic in context length. Below 32k the effect is within
run-to-run noise; beyond 64k it is unambiguous and large. Inspecting failing
traces, the errors concentrate on items whose reasoning chain crosses the
portion of the context held in the quantized cache: an early hop is recalled
slightly wrong, and the error propagates through the remaining hops.

5. ANALYSIS
We interpret the pattern as error COMPOUNDING. A single quantized key/value is
only slightly perturbed, but a multi-hop answer depends on several such recalls
in sequence; the probability that ALL hops survive quantization intact falls as
the chain lengthens and as more of the chain sits in quantized memory. This
predicts, correctly, that the loss grows with both context length and hop
count.

6. LIMITATIONS
We evaluated one model family and one (multi-hop) task distribution. We did not
test retrieval-style or single-hop tasks, and we did not vary the calibration
set size; both are left to future work. Our headline number (-9.2% at 128k) is
therefore specific to long multi-hop reasoning.

7. RELATED WORK
Weight-only quantization (e.g. 4-bit and 3-bit weights) is by now well studied
and often near-lossless; we do not touch weights here. KV-cache compression via
token eviction and via low-rank projection are orthogonal approaches to the
same memory problem; our result is specific to arithmetic quantization of the
retained cache. To our knowledge no prior work isolates the interaction between
KV precision and multi-hop chain length at 128k, which is our contribution.

8. ADDITIONAL RESULTS (by hop count, at 128k, 4-bit KV vs fp16 KV)
  - 4-hop items:  -6.0%
  - 6-hop items:  -9.4%
  - 8-hop items:  -12.3%
The loss grows with chain length at fixed context, consistent with the
compounding hypothesis (Section 5). Pooled across hop counts the figure is the
-9.2% headline. We also verified the effect is not a decoding artifact:
greedy and sampled decoding gave statistically indistinguishable deltas.

9. THREATS TO VALIDITY
(a) Single model family: we cannot rule out that a different architecture
tolerates 4-bit KV better. (b) Calibration set size: we used 512 documents;
a larger calibration set MIGHT reduce the loss, though our ablation from 256
to 512 moved the 128k figure by under 0.5 points, so we doubt calibration
alone explains a 9-point effect. (c) Task distribution: LR-Bench is entirely
multi-hop, so our result should not be read as a claim about retrieval tasks.

10. CONCLUSION
For long-context REASONING workloads, 4-bit KV quantization is unsafe: the
-9.2% accuracy cost at 128k is too large to accept. Use 8-bit KV beyond 32k.
We explicitly limit this claim to multi-hop reasoning; retrieval-style tasks
were not evaluated and may behave differently.

APPENDIX A.1 - HARDWARE AND SOFTWARE
All runs used a single 8-accelerator node. Inference used a paged-attention
server with the KV cache stored in the requested precision. Weights were held
at fp16 for every condition; only the KV cache precision varied. We fixed the
decoding temperature at 0 (greedy) for the headline table and repeated a subset
at temperature 0.7 to check robustness (Section 8). Each reported number is the
mean of three seeds; seed-to-seed spread on the 128k multi-hop cell was 0.6
points, so the -9.2% headline is well outside seed noise.

APPENDIX A.2 - QUANTIZATION DETAILS
We use symmetric, per-channel affine quantization of both keys and values, with
a group size of 128 along the head dimension. The scale for each channel is
fit on a calibration set of 512 held-out LR-Bench documents; we do NOT clip
outliers beyond the 99.9th percentile. We evaluated 8-bit and 4-bit; 2-bit was
unusable (it collapsed even at short context) and is omitted from the main
tables.

APPENDIX A.3 - FULL ACCURACY TABLE (LR-Bench multi-hop, exact match)
Rows are context length; columns are KV precision. Values are absolute EM.
  length | fp16  | 8-bit | 4-bit | (4-bit delta)
    8k   | 74.1  | 74.0  | 73.8  | -0.3
   16k   | 72.6  | 72.5  | 72.1  | -0.5
   32k   | 70.9  | 70.8  | 70.1  | -0.8
   64k   | 68.2  | 68.0  | 64.1  | -4.1
  128k   | 64.7  | 64.2  | 55.5  | -9.2
The 8-bit column is within half a point of fp16 at every length; the 4-bit
column tracks fp16 until 32k and then diverges. This is the central figure of
the paper: the divergence is a 4-bit-specific, length-dependent effect, not a
general quantization penalty (8-bit does not show it).

APPENDIX A.4 - PER-HOP-COUNT BREAKDOWN (4-bit delta vs fp16)
  hops |  32k  |  64k  | 128k
    4  | -0.4  | -2.6  |  -6.0
    6  | -0.9  | -4.3  |  -9.4
    8  | -1.2  | -6.1  | -12.3
At fixed context the loss grows with hop count; at fixed hop count it grows
with context. Both trends are predicted by the compounding hypothesis.

APPENDIX A.5 - ERROR ANALYSIS
We hand-inspected 120 failing 128k items. In 71% the model recalled an early
hop's entity slightly wrong (a near-neighbour in embedding space) and then
carried that error forward; in 18% it lost track of which of several similar
facts was the relevant one; the remaining 11% were formatting or refusal
errors unrelated to precision. The dominant mode is consistent with quantized
keys blurring fine distinctions between similar retrieved facts.

APPENDIX A.6 - REPRODUCIBILITY
We release the LR-Bench item ids and our calibration document ids. We do not
release model weights. The full table above is sufficient to reproduce the
headline: 64.7 -> 55.5 EM at 128k under 4-bit KV is the -9.2% we report.

APPENDIX A.7 - DECODING-TEMPERATURE ROBUSTNESS
To rule out a greedy-decoding artifact, we repeated the 128k multi-hop cell at
three temperatures (three seeds each):
  temperature | fp16 EM | 4-bit EM | delta
    0.0       |  64.7   |  55.5    | -9.2
    0.3       |  63.9   |  54.9    | -9.0
    0.7       |  62.1   |  53.2    | -8.9
The absolute accuracy falls with temperature (as expected) but the 4-bit
PENALTY is stable near -9 points across the sweep. The effect is a property of
the quantized cache, not of the decoder.

APPENDIX A.8 - MODEL-SIZE MINI-ABLATION
On a smaller 7B model (same window, same protocol) the 128k multi-hop 4-bit
penalty was -7.8%; on the 12B headline model it is -9.2%. The effect is present
at both sizes; we do not have a large enough sweep to claim a monotone trend in
model size, and we flag this as future work rather than a finding.

APPENDIX A.9 - EXTENDED ERROR TAXONOMY (128k multi-hop, 4-bit)
Of 240 hand-labelled failures:
  - 63%: wrong early-hop entity carried forward (compounding).
  - 15%: correct entities, wrong relation between them.
  - 9%:  right chain, wrong final normalization/format.
  - 8%:  retrieval of a plausible but wrong distractor fact.
  - 5%:  refusal / hedging without committing to an answer.
The dominance of the first category is the empirical core of the compounding
account: quantization most often corrupts an EARLY link, and the error then
propagates through the remaining hops. Retrieval-style single-hop items - which
have no downstream links to corrupt - are, by this account, far less exposed,
which is consistent with our decision to scope the -9.2% claim to multi-hop
reasoning and to make no claim about retrieval tasks.

documents.title: Paper B - CacheThrift (Nakamura & Ellis, 2026)
documents.content:
==================== PAPER B ====================
Title: CacheThrift: Aggressive KV Quantization at 128k Without Accuracy Loss
Authors: K. Nakamura, S. Ellis
Preprint: CT-2026-041 (2026)

ABSTRACT
Recent work has claimed that 4-bit KV-cache quantization breaks long-context
quality. We revisit the question with a broader calibration procedure and find
the opposite: 4-bit KV quantization is essentially lossless even at 128k
tokens, costing under 2% accuracy at maximum context. We conclude that 4-bit
KV is production-ready at long context and that the earlier negative result is
an artifact of a mis-tuned calibration set rather than a property of
quantization.

1. INTRODUCTION
KV-cache quantization promises large memory savings at long context. A recent
preprint (which we discuss in Section 5) reported severe degradation at 128k.
If true, that would rule out 4-bit KV for long-context serving. We could not
reproduce it, and this paper explains why we believe the effect is not real
for the workloads that matter to serving.

2. SETUP
We use a 13B base model with a 128k window. Our key methodological change from
prior work is the CALIBRATION SET: we calibrate the 4-bit KV quantizer on a
broad, activation-diverse corpus of 4,096 documents rather than a narrow set,
which we argue avoids over-fitting the quantization grid to an unrepresentative
activation range.

3. BENCHMARKS
We evaluate on two suites:
  (a) NIAH-Pro, a needle-in-a-haystack RETRIEVAL suite: a fact is planted in a
      long distractor context and the model must find and copy it.
  (b) A RETRIEVAL SUBSET of LR-Bench: we take only the single-hop, lookup-style
      items from LR-Bench and DELIBERATELY EXCLUDE its multi-hop items, in
      order to isolate retrieval behaviour from multi-step reasoning.

4. RESULTS
Accuracy delta of 4-bit KV vs fp16 KV at 128k context:
  - NIAH-Pro:                     -1.4%
  - LR-Bench (retrieval subset):  -2.3%
  - pooled headline:              -1.9%
Across context lengths from 8k to 128k the accuracy is flat to within noise;
we observe no monotonic degradation with length on either suite.

5. RELATION TO PRIOR WORK
We attempted to reproduce the large 128k degradation reported by the QuantLong
preprint (Paper A) and could not. We note that their calibration set was small
(512 documents) and hypothesise it over-fit a narrow activation range, which
would inflate quantization error on out-of-distribution activations. With our
broader calibration the effect vanishes. We do not have access to their exact
task items, but we believe calibration - not quantization per se - explains the
discrepancy.

6. LIMITATIONS
Our evaluation is retrieval-focused. We did not run a large multi-hop reasoning
suite; our LR-Bench usage was restricted to its retrieval subset by design. If
multi-hop reasoning behaves differently under quantization, our headline would
not capture it. We flag this as the main threat to external validity.

7. ADDITIONAL RESULTS (by context length, 4-bit KV vs fp16 KV, pooled)
  - 8k:    -0.6%
  - 32k:   -1.1%
  - 64k:   -1.5%
  - 128k:  -1.9%
The curve is nearly flat; the mild drift is within our reported noise band of
+/-0.7 points. We see no cliff of the kind reported by QuantLong at 64k-128k.

8. RELATED WORK
Our calibration procedure follows the broad-corpus recipe used for weight
quantization, adapted to the cache. Token-eviction methods trade recall of old
tokens for memory and are complementary to precision reduction; we do not
combine them here. We position our contribution as a careful reproduction that
isolates calibration quality as a confound in earlier KV-quantization results.

9. THREATS TO VALIDITY
(a) Retrieval focus: our benchmarks are retrieval/lookup-heavy by construction
(NIAH-Pro, and only the retrieval subset of LR-Bench). If multi-hop reasoning
degrades under 4-bit KV in a way retrieval does not, our headline would MISS
it; this is the single largest threat to the external validity of our
"production-ready" claim, and we flag it prominently. (b) One model family.
(c) We could not obtain the exact items used by QuantLong, so our calibration
explanation for their result remains a hypothesis rather than a demonstration.

10. CONCLUSION
4-bit KV quantization is production-ready at 128k: under 2% accuracy cost on
retrieval-style long-context tasks (headline -1.9%), with the earlier negative
result best explained, in our view, by calibration. We caution that our
evaluation did not include a large multi-hop reasoning suite.

APPENDIX B.1 - HARDWARE AND SOFTWARE
Runs used a single 8-accelerator node with a paged-attention server. Weights
were fp16; only KV precision varied. All numbers are the mean of three seeds;
seed spread was under 0.5 points on every cell, so the flatness we report is
not seed noise.

APPENDIX B.2 - CALIBRATION RECIPE (our key methodological change)
We calibrate the 4-bit KV quantizer on 4,096 activation-diverse documents
spanning code, prose, tables, and dialogue, deliberately broad so the
quantization grid is not fit to a narrow activation range. We use symmetric
per-channel quantization with a group size of 128, identical to prior work
EXCEPT for the calibration corpus. Our claim is that the corpus, not the
arithmetic, is what changed the outcome relative to earlier negative results.

APPENDIX B.3 - FULL ACCURACY TABLES (4-bit KV vs fp16 KV)
NIAH-Pro (retrieval, accuracy = fraction of needles correctly copied):
  length | fp16  | 4-bit | delta
    8k   | 99.1  | 98.8  | -0.3
   32k   | 98.4  | 98.0  | -0.4
   64k   | 97.6  | 96.9  | -0.7
  128k   | 96.2  | 94.8  | -1.4
LR-Bench retrieval subset (single-hop lookups, exact match):
  length | fp16  | 4-bit | delta
    8k   | 88.0  | 87.3  | -0.7
   32k   | 86.4  | 85.2  | -1.2
   64k   | 84.1  | 82.6  | -1.5
  128k   | 81.7  | 79.4  | -2.3
Pooling the two 128k rows by item count gives the -1.9% headline. Neither
suite shows a cliff between 64k and 128k.

APPENDIX B.4 - CALIBRATION-SIZE ABLATION
Holding the task set fixed (retrieval), we swept calibration size:
  calib docs | 128k delta (pooled)
     256     | -3.1
     512     | -2.6
    2,048    | -2.1
    4,096    | -1.9
A larger calibration corpus monotonically reduces the retrieval loss. We
argue this is why a small-calibration study would over-report the penalty on
retrieval tasks. (Whether it also explains a multi-hop penalty, we cannot say;
we did not run multi-hop.)

APPENDIX B.5 - DISCUSSION OF THE QUANTLONG RESULT
QuantLong (Paper A) reported -9.2% at 128k. We could not reproduce a loss of
that magnitude on our (retrieval) suites under any calibration size we tried;
our worst retrieval cell was -3.1% at the smallest calibration. Either their
result is calibration-driven, or it is task-driven in a way our retrieval-only
evaluation cannot see. We favoured the calibration explanation at the time of
writing; we acknowledge in Section 9 that a task-type explanation is possible.

APPENDIX B.6 - DEPLOYMENT NOTE
For serving teams: on retrieval-dominated long-context products (RAG fact
lookup, document search, needle-style extraction) our data support enabling
4-bit KV to 128k for the memory savings, with a monitored A/B on live traffic
as a safety net. We caution against generalising this to products whose long
prompts drive multi-step reasoning; that regime is outside our evaluation and,
per our own Section 9 threat, could behave differently. A single global toggle
("4-bit everywhere" or "4-bit nowhere") is likely the wrong abstraction; a
per-workload switch is safer given the uncertainty across task types.

documents.title: Paper C - PrecisionScaling (Okonkwo et al., 2026)
documents.content:
==================== PAPER C ====================
Title: PrecisionScaling: When KV-Cache Quantization Hurts Depends on the Task
Authors: A. Okonkwo, M. Baptiste, T. Renn
Preprint: PS-2026-077 (2026)

ABSTRACT
The literature disagrees on whether 4-bit KV-cache quantization is safe at long
context: one line of work reports severe degradation at 128k, another reports
almost none. We show the disagreement is largely explained by TASK TYPE. When a
long-context suite is partitioned into multi-hop reasoning items versus
retrieval (single-hop) items and evaluated at the same 128k context, 4-bit KV
degrades the reasoning items substantially (-7.4%) while barely touching the
retrieval items (-1.2%). We argue neither prior result is wrong; each measured a
different task mixture.

1. INTRODUCTION
Two recent preprints reach opposite headline conclusions about 4-bit KV at
128k. Rather than adjudicate which is correct, we ask whether both can be
correct on their own evaluation. We find they can, because the effect of
quantization is not uniform across task types.

2. METHOD
We evaluate a 13B model at a fixed 128k context on THREE partitions:
  - the multi-hop subset of LR-Bench (chained reasoning),
  - the retrieval (single-hop) subset of LR-Bench,
  - NIAH-Pro (retrieval).
We use a mid-sized calibration set (2,048 documents), between the sizes used by
the two prior works, so calibration size is unlikely to explain our contrasts.
All three partitions are run under identical conditions; only task type varies.

3. RESULTS
Accuracy delta of 4-bit KV vs fp16 KV at 128k:
  - LR-Bench multi-hop subset:   -7.4%
  - LR-Bench retrieval subset:   -1.2%
  - NIAH-Pro:                    -1.1%
The gap between the multi-hop and retrieval partitions (over 6 points) is far
larger than any calibration-driven difference we could induce by varying the
calibration set, which moved results by under 1 point in ablations.

4. RECONCILIATION
The apparent contradiction between QuantLong (Paper A) and CacheThrift (Paper B)
dissolves once task type is controlled. Paper A's headline of -9.2% used a
multi-hop suite; on our multi-hop partition we likewise see a large loss
(-7.4%). Paper B's near-zero result used predominantly RETRIEVAL tasks; on our
retrieval partitions we likewise see almost no loss (-1.1% to -1.2%). Neither
paper is simply wrong - they measured different mixtures of the same underlying
phenomenon. Calibration differences, the mechanism Paper B proposed, are real
but too small to account for the headline gap; task composition is the dominant
factor.

5. RECOMMENDATION
Choose KV precision by workload. Use 4-bit KV for retrieval-dominated
long-context workloads, where the cost is ~1%. Keep 8-bit KV where long
multi-hop reasoning dominates, where 4-bit costs several points. Mixed
workloads should budget for the reasoning fraction.

6. CALIBRATION ABLATION
Because a prior work (Paper B) attributed the disagreement to calibration, we
swept the calibration-set size across 512, 2,048, and 4,096 documents on both
partitions. The multi-hop loss moved from -7.9% to -7.4% to -7.1%; the
retrieval loss moved from -1.4% to -1.2% to -1.0%. In other words, calibration
size shifts results by well under one point, while the task-type gap is over
six points. Calibration is therefore a real but SECOND-ORDER effect; task type
is first-order. This is the crux of our reconciliation: Paper B was right that
calibration matters, but wrong that it explains the headline disagreement.

7. RELATED WORK
We build directly on QuantLong (Paper A) and CacheThrift (Paper B), reusing
LR-Bench and NIAH-Pro so our partitions are comparable to theirs. Orthogonal
lines of work (token eviction, low-rank KV, weight quantization) are out of
scope; we vary only KV precision and task type.

8. LIMITATIONS
We study one model size and report 128k as the headline. The qualitative
task-dependence held at 64k in a smaller ablation, but we report 128k for
comparability with prior work. We did not evaluate mixed single-prompt
workloads that interleave retrieval and reasoning; real applications may sit
between our partitions and should budget for their reasoning fraction.

APPENDIX C.1 - SETUP
A 13B model, paged-attention server, fp16 weights, KV precision varied. Three
seeds per cell; spread under 0.6 points. Symmetric per-channel 4-bit KV with a
group size of 128; calibration corpus of 2,048 documents unless the ablation
(Section 6) says otherwise.

APPENDIX C.2 - FULL TABLE (4-bit KV vs fp16 KV, at 128k)
  partition                     | fp16  | 4-bit | delta
  LR-Bench multi-hop subset     | 63.9  | 56.5  | -7.4
  LR-Bench retrieval subset     | 81.5  | 80.3  | -1.2
  NIAH-Pro                      | 96.0  | 94.9  | -1.1
The multi-hop and retrieval partitions differ by more than six points under
identical conditions. This is the paper's central result.

APPENDIX C.3 - RECONCILIATION TABLE
We line up each prior paper's headline against the partition it effectively
measured:
  claim source     | task mixture measured  | their 128k | our matching cell
  Paper A (-9.2%)  | multi-hop reasoning     |   -9.2%    | -7.4% (multi-hop)
  Paper B (-1.9%)  | retrieval / lookup      |   -1.9%    | -1.1% to -1.2% (retr.)
Both prior headlines fall on the SAME curve once you know which task mixture
produced them. The residual gap (e.g. -9.2% vs our -7.4% on multi-hop) is
within the range attributable to their smaller calibration set and item
differences; it does not change the qualitative picture.

APPENDIX C.4 - CALIBRATION VS TASK-TYPE (effect sizes)
Varying calibration size moved our results by < 1 point on either partition
(Section 6). Varying task type moved results by > 6 points. We therefore rank
task type as the first-order factor and calibration as second-order. A reader
should take away that BOTH prior papers were internally correct on their own
evaluations, and that the safe engineering rule is to pick KV precision by the
reasoning-vs-retrieval character of the workload, not by a single global number.

APPENDIX C.5 - TASK-DEPENDENCE ACROSS CONTEXT LENGTH (4-bit delta vs fp16)
To show the effect is not unique to 128k, we report both partitions across
lengths (a smaller run than the headline; three seeds):
  length | multi-hop | retrieval
    32k  |   -1.0    |   -0.4
    64k  |   -3.8    |   -0.8
   128k  |   -7.4    |   -1.2
The multi-hop/retrieval gap widens with context: negligible at 32k, several
points by 128k. This is the same qualitative shape QuantLong reported for its
(multi-hop) suite and the flatness CacheThrift reported for its (retrieval)
suite, now visible SIDE BY SIDE under one setup. The two prior results are
endpoints of a single task-dependent curve.

APPENDIX C.6 - PRACTITIONER GUIDANCE
- If your long-context workload is dominated by retrieval / lookup (RAG-style
  fact fetching, needle search), 4-bit KV is safe to ~128k (~1% cost).
- If it is dominated by long multi-hop reasoning (agentic chains, multi-step
  analysis over long inputs), budget several points of accuracy for 4-bit KV
  at 128k, or keep 8-bit KV for the reasoning-heavy fraction.
- Do not port a single global "4-bit is fine" or "4-bit is unsafe" verdict
  across workloads; the correct answer depends on the task mixture, which is
  exactly why the prior literature appeared to disagree.

APPENDIX C.7 - FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
Q: Does this mean QuantLong (Paper A) was wrong?
A: No. On the multi-hop partition we reproduce a large loss (-7.4%), close to
their -9.2%. Their result is correct FOR MULTI-HOP. Their error, if any, was
generalising a multi-hop number to "long context" broadly.
Q: Does this mean CacheThrift (Paper B) was wrong?
A: No. On retrieval we reproduce their near-zero loss (-1.1% to -1.2% vs their
-1.9%). Their result is correct FOR RETRIEVAL. Their error, if any, was
attributing the whole disagreement to calibration, which we find is a
second-order effect (< 1 point) next to task type (> 6 points).
Q: So who should I believe?
A: Both, scoped correctly. Believe A for multi-hop reasoning and B for
retrieval. The single number you should NOT trust is a task-agnostic "4-bit KV
costs X% at 128k"; there is no such workload-independent number.
Q: Is the residual gap (your -7.4% vs A's -9.2%) a contradiction?
A: No. It is within what item differences and A's smaller calibration set can
produce; both are "large multi-hop losses". The qualitative story is identical.

APPENDIX C.8 - SUMMARY FOR THE LITERATURE
The two prior headlines are not in conflict; they are two points on one
task-dependent curve, read off from two different task mixtures at the same
context length. Future work reporting KV-quantization results at long context
should state the reasoning-vs-retrieval composition of its evaluation, or the
number will be uninterpretable and will appear to "disagree" with studies that
chose a different mixture.

documents.title: Paper D - EvictLong (Sandoval & Weiss, 2026)
documents.content:
==================== PAPER D ====================
Title: EvictLong: Token Eviction Beats Precision Reduction for KV Memory
Authors: P. Sandoval, H. Weiss
Preprint: EL-2026-019 (2026)

ABSTRACT
We study a DIFFERENT lever for shrinking the KV cache: token EVICTION, which
drops low-attention past tokens entirely rather than storing every token at
lower precision. On a mix of summarization and QA tasks we show attention-guided
eviction recovers most of the memory savings of quantization while avoiding
arithmetic error. We do NOT quantize the cache; keys and values that are
retained are kept at fp16. This paper is about which tokens to keep, not about
the numeric precision of the ones you keep.

1. METHOD
We rank cached tokens by a running attention-mass estimate and evict the
lowest-ranked beyond a budget. Retained entries are unquantized.

2. RESULTS (accuracy delta vs full fp16 cache, at 128k)
  - eviction to 50% budget:  -0.5%
  - eviction to 25% budget:  -3.2%
  - eviction to 12% budget:  -8.8%
Memory scales with the budget; quality degrades gracefully as the budget
shrinks. These deltas are NOT comparable to precision-quantization results,
because the mechanism (dropping tokens) is different from reducing bit-width.

3. RELATION TO PRECISION QUANTIZATION
Eviction and quantization are orthogonal and can be combined. We deliberately
do not evaluate 4-bit or 8-bit KV precision in this work; comparisons to
precision-reduction papers would be apples-to-oranges. Readers interested in
the precision question should consult the KV-quantization literature directly.

4. RELATED WORK AND SCOPE
KV-memory reduction has two broad families: PRECISION reduction (quantizing the
stored keys/values to fewer bits) and CAPACITY reduction (storing fewer tokens,
via eviction, merging, or low-rank projection). This paper is squarely in the
capacity family. We do not evaluate any bit-width; every retained entry is
fp16. We therefore make no claim about 4-bit or 8-bit KV quantization, and our
numbers should NOT be placed on the same axis as precision-reduction studies -
the mechanisms trade off different resources and fail in different ways.

5. METHOD DETAIL
Our eviction policy maintains a running estimate of each cached token's
contribution to recent attention mass and evicts the lowest-contribution tokens
once the retained set exceeds a memory budget B (expressed as a fraction of the
full cache). Evicted tokens are gone; there is no dequantization step because
nothing was quantized.

6. FULL RESULTS (accuracy delta vs full fp16 cache, at 128k; summarization+QA)
  budget B | delta | retained memory
    75%    | -0.1  | 0.75x
    50%    | -0.5  | 0.50x
    37%    | -1.6  | 0.37x
    25%    | -3.2  | 0.25x
    12%    | -8.8  | 0.12x
Quality degrades gracefully as the budget shrinks. The knee is around 25%. We
stress again: these deltas measure the cost of DROPPING tokens, not of storing
them at lower precision, and are not comparable to quantization deltas.

7. THREATS TO VALIDITY
Our tasks are summarization and single-turn QA, not long multi-hop reasoning;
eviction may hurt multi-hop chains more (an evicted early fact cannot be
recovered). We did not test the interaction with precision reduction, which is
complementary and could be combined.

8. CONCLUSION
Attention-guided eviction is a competitive, precision-free route to KV memory
savings. Precision reduction is a separate axis and is explicitly out of scope
here; readers comparing techniques should not treat our eviction deltas as
quantization results.

documents.title: Benchmark Reference - LR-Bench & NIAH-Pro
documents.content:
==================== BENCHMARK REFERENCE ====================
Title: LR-Bench and NIAH-Pro: Benchmark Specifications (shared reference)
Note: this document describes the benchmarks used by the papers above. It
contains NO quantization results; it is background so that results in the
papers can be interpreted. Do not cite figures from this document as findings.

## LR-Bench (LongReason-Bench) v1.2
A long-context suite distributed at context lengths of 8k, 16k, 32k, 64k, and
128k tokens (each item is padded/extended with distractor text to the target
length). Scoring is exact match (EM) against a gold short answer.

LR-Bench is explicitly partitioned into two kinds of item:
  - MULTI-HOP items: the answer requires chaining 4 to 8 facts scattered across
    the context. These dominate the suite (about 70% of items) and are what the
    suite was designed to stress.
  - RETRIEVAL (single-hop) items: the answer is a single fact that must be
    located and copied; no chaining. These are the minority (about 30%).
A paper may report on the whole suite, or on just one subset. Because the two
subsets differ in difficulty and in what they stress, results are only
comparable across papers when the SAME subset is used. Readers should check
which subset each paper evaluated before comparing headline numbers.

## NIAH-Pro
A needle-in-a-haystack RETRIEVAL benchmark: a short "needle" fact is planted at
a random depth inside a long distractor "haystack" context, and the model must
find and reproduce it. Distributed at the same context lengths as LR-Bench.
Scoring is the fraction of needles reproduced exactly. NIAH-Pro is purely
retrieval; it contains no multi-hop reasoning items.

## Using these benchmarks to compare papers
Two studies that both say "we evaluated at 128k" may still be measuring very
different things if one used LR-Bench multi-hop and the other used NIAH-Pro or
the LR-Bench retrieval subset. When reconciling disagreements in the
literature, identify the task mixture each study measured, not just the context
length. The specifications here are stable across the cited papers; the papers
differ in which subset they chose to report.

## LR-Bench item statistics (v1.2)
  - total items: 4,800
  - multi-hop items: 3,360 (70%); hop-count distribution: 4 hops 35%, 5 hops
    25%, 6 hops 20%, 7 hops 12%, 8 hops 8%
  - retrieval (single-hop) items: 1,440 (30%)
  - gold answers are short spans (median 2 tokens); scoring is exact match
  - each item ships at all five context lengths (8k/16k/32k/64k/128k) via
    distractor padding that preserves the positions of the gold-relevant facts

## Example items (illustrative, abbreviated)
Multi-hop (LR-Bench): "Document mentions that the Orpheus project lead reports
to the VP named in section 3; that VP transferred from the division founded in
the year given in the appendix. In what year was that division founded?"
(Answer requires: find the lead -> find the VP -> find the VP's prior division
-> find that division's founding year: four hops.)
Retrieval (LR-Bench single-hop / NIAH-Pro): "What is the access code stored in
the maintenance note?" (Answer requires: locate one planted fact and copy it.)

## Notes for meta-analysis
Because the multi-hop subset both dominates LR-Bench (70%) and is far harder,
a study that reports "LR-Bench at 128k" without saying which subset is, by
default, reporting a multi-hop-heavy number. A study that says it used "the
retrieval subset of LR-Bench" or "NIAH-Pro" is reporting a retrieval number.
This document does not tell you how quantization affects either subset - see
the papers for that - but it tells you how to read which subset a paper used.

checker.rubric:
# Blind grading rubric - R2 (KV-quantization synthesis)

Score each numbered criterion independently: award the FULL points if it is
fully met, HALF if partially met, ZERO if not met or contradicted. Sum to a
total out of 10. Skeptic-first: list what is missing per criterion before
crediting it, and when torn, round down. This task PASSES iff total >= 7/10.

1. (2 pts) Identifies the core contradiction: Paper A concludes 4-bit KV
   quantization SIGNIFICANTLY degrades accuracy at long context (>=64k), while
   Paper B concludes it does NOT (essentially lossless at 128k).
2. (2 pts) Reports the headline figures with correct attribution: Paper A about
   -9% (-9.2%) at 128k, and Paper B about -2% (-1.9%, or its -1.4% / -2.3%
   components) at 128k. Wrong attribution or invented numbers => 0 for this item.
3. (2 pts) Identifies Paper C's reconciliation: the effect is TASK-DEPENDENT -
   multi-hop reasoning degrades (C: about -7.4% at 128k) while retrieval/NIAH
   barely moves (C: about -1.1% to -1.2%) - and that Paper A's result was
   multi-hop-heavy while Paper B's was retrieval-heavy.
4. (2 pts) Citation accuracy and no fabrication: every quantitative claim is
   attributable to one of the three provided papers; no invented figures,
   datasets, authors, or venues appear.
5. (2 pts) No overstatement: does not claim A and B actually agree, nor that C
   proves either A or B simply "wrong"; represents C's finding as
   task-dependence that reconciles both, not one paper refuting the other.
